coredata.dc:title: Ultra-high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their versatile application in fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Anzhong
Yang, Guanghui
Wan, Xuejuan
coredata.dc:description: The exploration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with ultra-high quantum yield, simple synthesis path, and satisfying output to facilitate their wide applications in numerous fields are always the research focus. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with strong blue fluorescence were synthesized with a simple one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, and the absolute quantum yield was as high as 92.1%. The detailed research results demonstrate that the N-CQDs have outstanding fluorescence stability, high selectivity, and anti-interference in Hg2+ detection. The obtained N-CQDs also possess excellent biocompatibility, which can also be successfully applied in cell imaging and intracellular Hg2+ detection. Most importantly, due to their high quantum yield and excellent dispersibility, the N-CQDs solution can be used as a quick-drying fluorescent ink for ink-jet printing. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CQDs have great potential in fluorescence sensing, biomedical diagnosis, data encryption, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent sensing
Bioimaging
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ions by N-CQDs
Optimization of synthesis conditions for N-CQDs
The morphology and surface structure characterization of N-CQDs
The fluorescence sensing of mercury ion with N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs with Hg2+
Cytotoxicity and bioimaging applications of N-CQDs
Application of inkjet printing fluorescent ink based on N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM images and (b) height curve of the N-CQDs. (c) TEM image (inset is the size distribution histogram) and (d) HRTEM images of the N-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of N-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra corresponding to C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d).

(a) The UV–vis (black curve), excitation (red curve), and emission (blue curve) spectra of N-CQDs; the inset shows a photograph of the N-CQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs under various excitation wavelengths.

(a) FL spectra of N-CQDs (pH 7.4 aqueous solutions) with different metal cations and (b) the corresponding digital photographs in UV light. (c) FL spectra of the N-CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0–10 μM). (d) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the N-CQDs (F/F 0) and various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–10 μM). The inset is a linear plotting of fluorescence quenching rate (F 0-F)/F 0 versus the concentrations of Hg2+. (e) Relative fluorescence intensities (F/F 0) of the N-CQDs aqueous solutions with 20 μM of various metal ions (black bar) and the following treatment of 10 μM Hg2+ (red bar).

(a) AFM image of the N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CQDs, N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+ and 10 μM Hg2+ solution. (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the N-CQDs and N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+ upon excitation at 370 nm. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CQDs, N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+, and N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+ and saturated EDTA solution (the insets are the photographs under UV light).

(a) MTT assay of NIH 3T3 fibroblast cells after 12 and 24 h of incubation with various concentrations of N-CQDs. The CLSM fluorescence images of PC-3 living cells which were incubated with N-CQDs (40 μg/mL) for 3 h (b), the treated cells were incubated with Hoechst stocking medium (5 μg/mL) for 10 min (c) and overlay image (d). (e-g) The CLSM fluorescence images of PC-3 cells were pretreated with N-CQDs and Hg2+.
Daylight (a-c) and 365 nm UV light (d-f) inkjet printed sample photographs: (a, d) QR code, (b, e) emblem of Shenzhen University, and (c, f) English characters.

head.ce:title: Ultra-high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their versatile application in fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology
Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with ultra-high quantum yield, simple synthesis path, and satisfying output to facilitate their wide applications in numerous fields are always the research focus. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with strong blue fluorescence were synthesized with a simple one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, and the absolute quantum yield was as high as 92.1%. The detailed research results demonstrate that the N-CQDs have outstanding fluorescence stability, high selectivity, and anti-interference in Hg2+ detection. The obtained N-CQDs also possess excellent biocompatibility, which can also be successfully applied in cell imaging and intracellular Hg2+ detection. Most importantly, due to their high quantum yield and excellent dispersibility, the N-CQDs solution can be used as a quick-drying fluorescent ink for ink-jet printing. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CQDs have great potential in fluorescence sensing, biomedical diagnosis, data encryption, and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely studied since they were discovered in 2004[1]. Compared with organic fluorescent molecules and semiconductor quantum dots [2,3], CQDs exhibit both excellent optical stability and good biocompatibility, which can be easily prepared from widely sourced raw materials with low cost [4–8]. But according to the previous literature, the quantum yield of the reported CQDs are generally low, which will limit their practical application [9,10].

To improve the quantum yield, researchers have conducted a lot of trial works. There are generally two strategies to adjust the fluorescence properties of the CQDs. The first one relies on the selection of different synthesis methods, including electrochemical oxidation [11], ultrasonic treatment [12], laser ablation [13], and microwave-assisted heating [14]. These methods mentioned regularly require the use of organic solvents and strong oxidants to modify or passivate the CQDs. By comparison, one-pot hydrothermal synthesis has become the most attractive approach because of its simple operation and easy control during the reaction [15,16]. Heteroatom doping (nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, boron) is another widely utilized strategy to improve the quantum yield of the CQDs, while nitrogen doping or co-doping of nitrogen with other atoms are considered to be the most effective way [17–20]. The heteroatoms doping can change the internal structure or surface state of CQDs, thereby adjust their energy gap width and electron density, which is beneficial to improve their quantum yield. For example, Zhang et al. prepared N, S co-doped CQDs with a quantum yield as high as 65% using citric acid and L-cysteine as raw materials [21]. Yang and coworkers reported N, P co-doped CQDs with a quantum yield of 38% through microwave-assisted method [22]. Shen et al. selected boric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials to successfully introduce boron atoms into CQDs, obtaining a high quantum yield of 54%[23].

Besides, expanding the application of CQDs in the diverse field is also a common concern of researchers, especially in fluorescence sensing, imaging, and fluorescence anti-counterfeiting [24–29]. Compared to the widely reported organic fluorescence molecules and semiconductor quantum dots with either poor stability, unsatisfactory water dispersibility, or great toxicity, the comprehensive advantages of CQDs, such as excellent fluorescence stability, low toxicity, good water dispersion, low cost, and easy synthesis, endowed them with unique application potential both in the fluorescent sensing and imaging [30–32]. However, it must be pointed out that most of the current CQD probes generally have unsatisfactory quantum yield, that their fluorescence sensing performance could be improved. At the same time, CQDs-based fluorescent inks to achieve anti-counterfeiting function gradually aroused the interest of researchers along with the increasing anti-counterfeiting demand of commodities, valuable documents, and currency in daily life. Compared with the commercial anti-counterfeiting mechanism of temperature response, magnetic response, and laser holographic response, fluorescent printing has attracted a lot of research interest due to its advantages of simple operation and easy verification. At present, most of the fluorescent inks are achieved by using organic dyes, rare earth materials, and semiconductor quantum dots [33–35]. However, the high price, poor biocompatibility, and environmental hazards could limit their application in the food and medical fields. Therefore, CQDs are considered to be promising new materials for optical anti-counterfeiting [36,37].

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a simple and economical one-pot hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CQDs emit intense blue fluorescence, and the absolute quantum yield could be as high as 92.1%. Among the diverse metal ions, only Hg2+ could cause the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs, indicating that N-CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe to detected Hg2+. The experimental results showed that N-CQDs exhibited high selectivity, sensitivity, and good anti-interference performance in the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+. Moreover, N-CQDs have been successfully utilized in cell imaging and intracellular detection of Hg2+ due to their good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Because of their high quantum yield and fluorescence stability, N-CQDs aqueous solution could be also utilized as the fluorescence ink for the inkjet printer, while the information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications could be easily achieved by the printing of various fluorescent patterns.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without purification. Citric acid monohydrate (CA), o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA), sodium nitrate, potassium nitrate, cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, chromium nitrate nonahydrate, lead nitrate, nickel nitrate hexahydrate, calcium nitrate tetrahydrate, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate, iron nitrate nonahydrate, aluminum nitrate nonahydrate, copper nitrate trihydrate, magnesium perchlorate hexahydrate, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium chloride and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mercury perchlorate trihydrate was supplied by Strem Chemicals, Inc. The Milli-Q microporous system was used to prepare ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ ·cm) used in the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) testing was performed on an FEI Titan Cubed Themis G2 300. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images were obtained by Bruker Dimension Icon microscope. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Miniflex600 X-ray diffractometer. The fluorescence spectra were attained by the Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Nicolet 6700. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Microlab 350 X-ray electron spectrometer with focused monochromatized Al Kα radiation (10 KeV). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by an FL-TCSPC spectrophotometer. The absolute quantum yield was obtained on a Hamamatsu Quantaurus-QY C11347 spectrometer. The size distribution and zeta potential were obtained on the Malvern Nano ZSP instrument at room temperature. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were observed on an Olympus FV1000 microscope. The fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink was printed by using an inkjet printer HP DeskJet 2132. Paper with weak fluorescence intensity purchased from Youtai Corporation was used for printing.

The N-CQDs were prepared by using CA and o-PDA as raw material. In brief, CA (0.210 g, 1 mmol) and o-PDA (0.216 g, 2 mmol) were added to 10 mL of ultrapure water and dissolved at 60 ℃ under agitation. After that, the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 ℃ for 8 h. After the reaction, the reaction kettle was naturally cooled to room temperature. A brown aqueous solution of the crude product was firstly centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered to remove large or agglomerated particles with a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The obtained solution of N-CQDs was further dialyzed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 12 h to remove unreacted small molecular species. Finally, the N-CQDs were obtained by freeze-drying with a yield of 16.8% and stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

The absolute quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following equation [38]:
(1)
QY =
∫
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
s o l v e n t
- ∫
E
s a m p l e
where QY is the absolute quantum yield, Lemission is the number of fluorescent emission photons of the N-CQDs, Esolvent and Esample are photon numbers of ultrapure water and the N-CQDs excited by excitation light source, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ions by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions stock solutions (1.0 mM) of Na+, K+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ were prepared in ultrapure water. Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs were recorded under the excitation at 370 nm. First, 2.0 mL of N-CQDs solution (10 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH = 7.4) was mixed with various volumes of Hg2+ stocked solution in centrifuge tubes, and then diluted to 3 mL. The mixed solutions were allowed to react for 1 h at room temperature, and then, the fluorescence spectra were measured. The selectivity of N-CQDs was confirmed by mixing N-CQDs with other metal ions instead of Hg2+ in a similar way. The concentrations of Hg2+ and other metal ions mentioned above are both 10 μM. For the interference tests, the N-CQDs solution was mixed with Hg2+ in the presence and absence of other interfering metal ions. The interfering ions and Hg2+ were added at a concentration ratio of 2:1.

Tap water collected in our laboratory and lake water taken from Xixing Lake on the Lihu campus of Shenzhen University were utilized for the real sample analysis. The water sample were centrifuged at high speed (12000 rpm, 10 min), and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter before use [39,40]. The fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ions in these real samples are conducted the same as obove-mentioned.

The NIH 3T3 fibroblast cells were used to determine the cytotoxicity of N-CQDs by the standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) method. The NIH 3T3 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates 24 h before use. Then freeze-dried N-CQDs at different concentrations (0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL) were incubated with the cells at 37 °C for 12 or 24 h. Then added 20 μL MTT (0.5%) and incubated the cells for 4 h. After washing three times with PBS, added 100 μL of DMSO to dissolve MTT. Using a microplate reader to check the optical density value (OD) at 490 nm at last. Cell viability was calculated according to the following equation [41]:
(2)
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
%
=
O
D
N - C Q D s
O
D
Control
where ODN-CQDs is the test result after the addition of N-CQD and cell co-incubation, and ODControl is the test result of the control group without adding the N-CQDs.

To obtain the cell imaging capacity of N-CQDs, the PC-3 cells were seeded in a 1 × 105 density petri dish at 37 °C and cultured for 24 h under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The freeze-dried N-CQDs were dispersed into Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM) as stocking medium (40 μg/mL). Then incubate the cells under the same conditions as above. After incubated for 3 h, cells were washed with PBS 3 times, and then the cells were incubated with Hoechst stocking medium (5 μg/mL) for 10 min to stain the nuclei. After that, the PC-3 cells were observed with CLSM. To study the ability of intracellular Hg2+ detection of N-CQDs, cells first undergo the staining process consistent with the above experiments. Then the cells were washed 3 times with PBS to remove the redundant Hoechst. The washed cells were then incubated with Hg2+ stocking medium (10 μM) for 10 min. After that, the treated cells were observed in the same way as above.

The N-CQDs powder (1 mg) was dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min and dispersed evenly. The prepared fluorescent ink was packaged in a blank ink cartridge of an inkjet printer with a syringe. After the inkjet printer was connected to a computer, various patterns could be printed on A4 paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen-doped quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using CA and o-PDA as the raw materials which contain abundant carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. Nitrogen atoms in o-PDA can realize the nitrogen doping of CQDs, which can help to improve the QY value of CQDs. The raw materials were put into the reactor with high temperature and high pressure, a polymer-like material was first formed through a condensation polymerization, which was further carbonized to form the N-CQDs. Extensive studies have shown that the fluorescence properties of CQDs are strongly affected by the synthesis technical. Thus, varied reaction conditions such as reaction temperatures, reaction time, and the mole ratio of o-PDA and CA were investigated to optimize the fluorescence performance of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-CQDs was first increased and then decreased with the increasing reaction time, temperature, and the mole ratio of o-PDA/CA. The phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that with the increasing reaction temperature and time, more carbon cores can be obtained to form the N-CQDs, while the appropriate o-PDA/CA molar ratio is more conducive to form fluorophore. If the reaction temperature and time continued to increase, the formation rate and the particle size of the carbon cores will increase, which will reduce the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs [42]. The N-CQDs showed the strongest fluorescence intensity when the mole ratio of o-PDA and CA was 2:1 with a reaction time of 8 h at 160℃. Under such conditions, the quantum yield of the prepared N-CQDs was as high as 92.1%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and surface structure characterization of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and surface structure of the N-CQDs were characterized in detail. AFM images showed that the height of the obtained N-CQDs was about 0.5–2 nm (Fig. 1 a and b). Meanwhile, TEM images (Fig. 1c and Fig. S2) showed that the prepared N-CQDs were spherical dots with an average particle size of 3.0 ± 0.8 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. 1d) showed that their interplanar distance was 0.31 nm, which was corresponded to the (002) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [43]. Moreover, a broad peak around 22.3° could be observed in the XRD spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. S3a), which was characteristic of amorphous carbon. The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs was also shown in Fig. S3b. The broadband at 3310–3550 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The peak at 2928 cm−1 was assigned to the C-H bonds. The peak at 1639 cm−1 could be attributed to the CO stretching vibration. And the peaks at 1271, 1402, 1582, and 1621 cm−1 were ascribed to C-O-C, C-N, CC, and CN stretching vibrations respectively [44,45].

XPS technology was utilized to confirm the nitrogen doping and analyze the surface composition of N-CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, three intense peaks at 284.8, 400.4, and 531.7 eV of the XPS spectrum were assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The elemental analysis (Table S1) exhibited that the N-CQDs were composed with C atom of 69.8%, N atom of 15.16% and O atom of 15.04%. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) revealed three peaks at 284.6, 285.6, and 287.2 eV, which were corresponding to CC/CC, CN, and CO groups. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 2c) showed three peaks at 398.8, 400.2, and 401.0 eV, which were ascribed to the CNC, N-(C)3, and N-H groups. The spectra of O1s (Fig. 2d) exhibited two peaks at 531.3 and 533.0 eV, which were attributed to the CO and CO groups [46,47]. The XPS and FTIR data proved that nitrogen atoms were successfully doped in N-CQDs, and the surface of the N-CQDs had abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs were investigated. The absorption peaks of N-CQDs at 272 nm and 278 nm (Fig. 3a) were attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN bonds in the aromatic rings, while the absorption peak at 370 nm corresponded to the n–π* transition of the CO bond [48]. The excitation and fluorescence emission spectra exhibited that N-CQDs had the maximum excitation and emission peaks at 370 nm and 440 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3a inset, the diluted aqueous solution of N-CQDs was colorless and transparent in daylight, but it could emit strong blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. The N-CQDs also possessed excellent chemical and optical stability (Fig. S4), which was stable for more than 30 days and had good resistance to the light bleaching (irradiated at 365 nm for 60 min, 6 W) and the salt solution with high concentration. The N-CQDs also exhibited an excitation-independent fluorescence emission, that the maximum fluorescence intensity occurred at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was also tested at various pH values (3 to 9), and the results displayed that the fluorescence intensity was the strongest at pH 7 (Fig. S5). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at physiological pH was also tested, which was slightly lower than that at pH 7 (Fig. S6). Thus, the following fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, and anti-counterfeiting applications were all conducted at physiological pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence sensing of mercury ion with N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity toward Hg2+ ions (Fig. 4 ). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs displayed a slight increase after the addition of various metal ions, including Na+, K+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ (Fig. 4a). However, a conspicuous fluorescence quenching occurred after the addition of Hg2+, which can be directly observed by the naked eye under the UV lamp (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence response time of the N-CQDs toward 10 μM Hg2+ was also tested (Fig. S7). The results revealed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs declined rapidly in the first 10 min after the addition of Hg2+, which reached a stable equilibrium in about 60 min at room temperature. A fluorescence titration experiment was then utilized to investigate the detection sensitivity of the N-CQDs probe. Different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–10 μM) were then added to the N-CQDs solution, and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured after 1 h. Fig. 4c clearly showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs decreased with the increasing concentration of Hg2+. Fig. 4d demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching ratio, (F 0-F)/F 0, displayed a credible linear correlation (R2 = 0.997) with Hg2+ concentrations of 0.3–5 μM, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs at 440 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The linear equation is described as (F0 -F)/F0 = 0.1776C-0.0549, where C refers to the concentration of Hg2+. The limit of the determination (LOD) was calculated to be 24.1 nM. The LOD was calculated based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the 10 detections for the blank sample test and s is the slope of the concentration-dependent curve [49,50]. Comparing N-CQDs with other Hg2+ probes based on carbon quantum dots (Table S2), the preparation process of N-CQDs is simple with low cost and higher quantum yield. The anti-interference evaluation of the fluorescence detection process was also carried out, that the fluorescence emission intensity ratio changes, F/F 0, were recorded for bare N-CQDs (blue), N-CQDs with various metal cations (black), and N-CQDs with various metal cations and Hg2+ (red). As can be seen in Fig. 4e, the existence of numerous metal cations exhibited negligible impact during the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+, indicated that N-CQDs had excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ with strong anti-interference. Tap water and lake water were utilized to evaluate the practicality of N-CQDs for Hg2+ detection in real environmental samples. The recovery rate and relative standard deviation value of the fluorescence sensing for Hg2+ were listed in Table S3, indicating that the obtained N-CQDs probe exhibited relatively good accuracy and reliability, which has a potential application prospect in the detection of heavy metal ions in the actual environment [51–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs with Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of CQDs can be caused either by the dynamic quenching of energy and electron transfer or by the static quenching of the non-fluorescence complex formed in the ground state. To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs toward Hg2+, the morphology and the surface state of the N-CQDs with and without Hg2+ ions were investigated in detail. The zeta potential of the N-CQDs was shown to be −45.5 mV (Fig. S8), which could be ascribed to the ionization of the hydroxyl or carboxyl group on the surface of the N-CQDs. This means that electrostatic interaction can occur between the N-CQDs and Hg2+, which provides a theoretical basis for the formation of the N-CQDs-Hg2+ complex. The AFM image revealed that the N-CQDs had an obvious aggregation after adding Hg2+ (Fig. 5 a). DLS was also used to compare the size distribution of the N-CQDs solution before and after Hg2+ addition. The test results displayed that the size distribution of the N-CQDs solution after Hg2+ addition could reach hundreds of nanometers (Fig. S9), indicating that the increase of non-radiative transition caused by aggregation may be one of the reasons for fluorescence quenching [54,55]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum can further verify the fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ to the N-CQDs. Fig. 5b showed that the absorption peak intensity of the N-CQDs at 370 nm decreased significantly and exhibited a slight red shift after the addition of Hg2+. According to the previous reports, the shift of the absorption peak can be attributed to the structural change caused by the combination of Hg2+ with N-CQDs [56].

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was further investigated by the fluorescence decay measurement, and the results were recorded in Fig. 5c and Table S4. All tested fluorescence decay curves could be well fitted with double exponential functions. The average lifetime τavg of N-CQDs with and without Hg2+ was determined by the following equation [57]:
(3)
τ
avg
=
B 1
τ
1
2
+
B 2
τ
2
2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
where B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors, τ1 and τ2 are the decay times. The average fluorescence lifetime of the pure N-CQDs was 9.31 ns, while those of the N-CQDs with 10 μM Hg2+ ions reduced to 9.18 ns, indicating that there is a static quenching process between Hg2+ and the N-CQDs [58,59]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs caused by Hg2+ ions could almost be recovered after the addition of EDTA, indicating that EDTA has a higher affinity toward Hg2+ than N-CQDs, and the N-CQDs-Hg2+ complex is disintegrated [60]. All the above results indicated that the fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs by Hg2+ was static quenching, which was attributed to the interaction between Hg2+ and the surface COOH/OH groups on N-CQDs, and the resulting electron transfer finally led to the non-radiative electron/hole recombination and fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and bioimaging applications of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the fluorescence ability of the N-CQDs toward Hg2+ was evaluated at the cellular level. First, the cytotoxicity of the N-CQDs in NIH 3T3 fibroblasts was evaluated by MMT assay. The results showed that the cell viability remained 90% even after 24 h incubation with 100 μg/mL N-CQDs, indicating that the synthesized N-CQDs had low cytotoxicity (Fig. 6 a). As shown in Fig. 6b, the bright green fluorescence was observed from the CLSM image after incubating the N-CQDs with the cells for 3 h and the fluorescence is mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm. For better differentiation, the Hoechst nuclear stain was used to treat the nucleus staining (Fig. 6c) and the overlay image was shown in Fig. 6d. It was obvious that N-CQDs exhibited no significant effect on the cell morphology, indicating that N-CQDs could be used in intracellular fluorescence imaging and sensing. Fig. 6e–g showed that after adding 10 μM Hg2+ to the cells pre-cultured with N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity in the cytoplasm became very weak, suggesting that N-CQDs could be successfully utilized in the qualitative detection of Hg2+ in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of inkjet printing fluorescent ink based on N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the high quantum yield, strong fluorescence stability, and good dispersibility of N-CQDs, we had developed a fluorescent ink for printing with the as-prepared N-CQDs, which showed full compatibility with commercially available inkjet printers. When used for printing on paper, the N-CQDs have various polar functional groups on the surface, which can be well attached to paper fibers and then quickly dried. As shown in Fig. 7 , various complex patterns were quickly and easily obtained through inkjet printing, including QR code, the emblem of Shenzhen University, and English characters. The printed pattern (Fig. 7a–c) was completely invisible to the naked eye under daylight, while the corresponding blue fluorescent patterns were observed under ultraviolet lamps (Fig. 7d–f). It is worth mentioning that the blue fluorescent QR code can be easily identified by a smartphone to obtain hidden information, indicating that the fluorescence pattern obtained by inkjet printing has good clarity and accuracy. Moreover, we also tried to print the designed QR code on the banknote with fluorescent ink as shown in Fig. S10. It could be observed that there was almost no trace on the banknote under the daylight, while the clear blue fluorescent patterns were obtained under the UV lamp, which further demonstrates the feasibility of expanding N-CQDs fluorescence ink to practical applications. The above results illustrate that N-CQDs with high fluorescence intensity, low cost, and low toxicity may have great potential in fluorescent printing, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption applications.

In summary, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with ultra-high quantum yield were prepared by using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials through a convenient hydrothermal method. The quantum yield of N-CQDs obtained can be as high as 92.1% with excellent fluorescence stability. The research results indicated that Hg2+ could cause the aggregation of N-CQDs, which induced the fluorescence quenching of the nanoparticle. The obtained N-CQDs were proved to be an excellent fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ with good selectivity, sensitivity, and anti-interference performance. It has also been proved that the detection system can be used to detect Hg2+ in real water samples, and the recoveries were 108.0%-116.0%. Besides, it is worth noting that N-CQDs exhibit low toxicity and good biocompatibility, and have been successfully used in cell imaging and intracellular Hg2+ detection, suggesting that N-CQDs possess great potential in the field of environmental detection and biomedical diagnosis in the future. However, the response time of N-CQDs toward Hg2+ ion is relatively long, which remained to be improved. At the same time, the as-prepared N-CQDs with high quantum yield and excellent water solubility were used as fluorescent inks. The desired images and information can be easily printed through inkjet printing, which may provide new ideas for their future applications in optical anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

Anzhong Tan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guanghui Yang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Xuejuan Wan: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21875144, 21802097), Shenzhen Science and Technology Research Grant (JCYJ20200109105003940) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2018A030313758) is gratefully acknowledged. We also acknowledge the technique of support from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Shenzhen University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119583.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.J. Ren, Y.X. Meng, Q. Zhang, M.Y. Tang, B.Y. Zhu, F. Chai, et al., Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the detection of mercury ions in living cells and visualization of latent fingerprints, New J. Chem. 42 (2018) 6824-6830.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step, stabilizer-free and green synthesis of Cu nanoclusters as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of nitrite ions
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.J. Zheng, R.P. Liang, Z.J. Li, L. Zhang, J.D. Qiu, One-step, stabilizer-free and green synthesis of Cu nanoclusters as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of nitrite ions, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 230 (2016) 314-319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
Sens. Actuators B Chem.
